segments.title: A Tiger memoir and the production question
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke introduces a Tiger-tank memoir while questioning whether Germany would have gained more from producing improved Panzer IVs in greater numbers.
segments.body: The review begins from a force-design objection rather than enthusiasm for the Tiger and King Tiger. Dr. Clarke contrasts their resource demands with the possibility of building and up-gunning more Panzer IVs, framing the memoir against the wider wartime problem of quality versus quantity in armoured production.

evidence.note: The introduction identifies the book and contrasts Tiger production with more Panzer IVs.

segments.title: Why Poznań repays attention
segments.summary: The review makes the case for a less familiar battle and for a memoir that adds a German tank crewman's view to broader studies of armoured warfare.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes Poznań as a battle that receives uneven attention, then explains why the book caught his interest despite his initial reluctance. He situates it beside the national traditions of tank history he has read, noting how accounts of commanders and vehicles can shape the story a reader takes away from a campaign.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke calls Poznań an unevenly studied battle and discusses different national approaches to tank history.

segments.title: Life inside the besieged citadel
segments.summary: A passage from the memoir shows how crowded shelter, medical shortages, airborne supply, and Soviet bombardment shaped the defence of Poznań's citadel.
segments.body: The memoir excerpt turns the citadel into more than a strongpoint: it is a supply depot, casualty station, and refuge under constant fire. It also shows how the defenders depended on parachute supply drops and how a Tiger could be disabled by the combined effects of bombardment, fire, and mechanical strain even after being saved from immediate destruction.

evidence.note: The quoted account describes the citadel's overcrowding, air supply, bombardment, and the Tiger's damaged clutch.

segments.title: Breakout attempts and the final defence
segments.summary: The account follows the shrinking German perimeter, a night breakout, and the medical and supply collapse around the citadel's remaining defensive positions.
segments.body: The extract follows troops attempting to escape through the Soviet encirclement after the eastern bridgehead becomes untenable, while the remaining fight contracts around the citadel. A Tiger's favourable field of fire cannot solve the wider shortage of medical care, food, and relief for the wounded, making this a vivid illustration of how siege conditions limit tactical advantages.

evidence.note: The memoir recounts the night breakout, the citadel's last positions, and overwhelmed medical arrangements.

segments.title: A worthwhile memoir with an uncomfortable context
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke recommends the memoir as a strong first-hand account while keeping its crew's experience and Nazi Germany's criminal war in view together.
segments.body: The closing assessment says the book supplied an unexpected perspective and is a good choice for readers seeking a first-hand account of the battle. Dr. Clarke returns to the Tiger production trade-off, then distinguishes the human experience of a crew fighting for its country from approval of the regime and its actions. That distinction is essential when using personal military memoirs as historical evidence.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke evaluates the book, revisits the production question, and discusses the moral context of the account.
